coredata.dc:title: pH-responsive intramolecular FRET-based self-tracking polymer prodrug nanoparticles for real-time tumor intracellular drug release monitoring and imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Dong, Yuman
Du, Pengcheng
Liu, Peng
coredata.dc:description: An intramolecular fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET)-based macromolecular theranostic prodrug was designed by directly conjugating Doxorubicin (DOX) as the FRET acceptor onto the naphthalimide side groups in the fluorescent copolymer PPEGMA20-PNAP8 as the FRET energy donor via an acid-labile imine bond, without a fluorogenic linker. The proposed PPEGMA20-PNAP8-DOX theranostic prodrug showed a high DOX content of 24.3% owing to a conjugation efficiency of > 93% under mild conjugation conditions. It could easily self-assemble into unique theranostic nanoparticles with a Dh of 71 nm. The theranostic nanoparticles showed excellent pH-triggered DOX release performance with very low premature drug leakage of 6.3% in normal physiological medium over 129 h, while>91% of the conjugated DOX was released in the acidic tumor intracellular microenvironment. MTT assays indicated the enhanced antitumor efficacy of the proposed theranostic nanoparticles compared with free DOX. Furthermore, because drug release was triggered by pH, orange fluorescence was restored to the blue fluorescence of the backbone copolymer. Such self-tracking pH-responsive colorful fluorescence variations during intracellular drug delivery and release are expected to allow real-time tumor intracellular drug release monitoring and imaging diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tumor theranostics
Polymer prodrug nanoparticles
Intramolecular FRET
Self-tracking
pH-responsive

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the polymer prodrug PPEGMA20-PNAP8-DOX
In vitro pH-triggered DOX release from PPEGMA20-PNAP8-DOX theranostic nanoparticles
In vitro cellular toxicity and uptake

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: 1H NMR spectra of (a) PPEGMA20-PNAP8 in CDCl3 and (b) PPEGMA20-PNAP8-DOX in DMSO‑d 6.
TEM image (a) and hydrodynamic diameters (b) of the PPEGMA20-PNAP8-DOX nanoparticles.
In vitro DOX release profiles in pH 5.0 and pH 7.4 buffer solutions (a) and hydrodynamic diameters in pH 5.0 buffer solution at different time.

(a) Emission spectra of the PPEGMA20-PNAP8 (0.7 mg/mL) solution in DMSO before and after adding DOX (5 mg/mL); (b) Emission spectra of the PPEGMA20-PNAP8-DOX nanoparticles (0.5 mg/mL) in pH 5.0 ABS buffer after different time; (c) fluorescence intensity of the PPEGMA20-PNAP8 in different pH buffer solutions; excitation wavelength: 380 nm.

Cytotoxicity of PPEGMA20-PNAP8, PPEGMA20-PNAP8-DOX and free DOX on the HepG2 cells after incubation for 24 h (a), Cytotoxicity of PPEGMA20-PNAP8-DOX and free DOX on the HepG2 cells after incubation for 48 h (b) and 72 h (c), respectively. The data are given as the mean ± SD (n = 3).

Fluorescence images of the HepG2 cells after incubation with PPEGMA20-PNAP8-DOX (15 μg/mL) for 24 h (a, b and c stained with DAPI, d and e without DAPI). The numbers a and d, b, c and e represent the images of the channels of DOX, DAPI, NAP, the a + b, a + c and d + e were the overlapped images of a with b, a with c, and d with e, respectively.

ce:title.ce:sup: pH-responsive intramolecular FRET-based self-tracking polymer prodrug nanoparticles for real-time tumor intracellular drug release monitoring and imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An intramolecular fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET)-based macromolecular theranostic prodrug was designed by directly conjugating Doxorubicin (DOX) as the FRET acceptor onto the naphthalimide side groups in the fluorescent copolymer PPEGMA20-PNAP8 as the FRET energy donor via an acid-labile imine bond, without a fluorogenic linker. The proposed PPEGMA20-PNAP8-DOX theranostic prodrug showed a high DOX content of 24.3% owing to a conjugation efficiency of > 93% under mild conjugation conditions. It could easily self-assemble into unique theranostic nanoparticles with a Dh of 71 nm. The theranostic nanoparticles showed excellent pH-triggered DOX release performance with very low premature drug leakage of 6.3% in normal physiological medium over 129 h, while>91% of the conjugated DOX was released in the acidic tumor intracellular microenvironment. MTT assays indicated the enhanced antitumor efficacy of the proposed theranostic nanoparticles compared with free DOX. Furthermore, because drug release was triggered by pH, orange fluorescence was restored to the blue fluorescence of the backbone copolymer. Such self-tracking pH-responsive colorful fluorescence variations during intracellular drug delivery and release are expected to allow real-time tumor intracellular drug release monitoring and imaging diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:para: Doxorubicin (DOX), one of the most widely used chemotherapeutics, shows wide-spectrum anticancer activity against several malignancies. However, its clinical application is limited by toxic side-effects due to its nonspecificity (Cagel et al., 2017). In recent decades, smart nanoscale drug delivery systems (DDSs) that endow tumor-specific drug release have been developed (Aftab et al., 2018). In particular, polymer prodrugs, in which chemotherapeutic molecules are conjugated onto the polymer backbone with various dynamic covalent bonds, such as acid-labile linkages (imine, hydrazone or acetal bond) (Pang et al., 2016) and bioreducible disulfide bonds (Sun et al., 2018), have attracted increasing interest owing to their on-demand drug delivery performance (Seidi et al., 2018).

Such polymer prodrugs are more stable in normal physiological medium and show much lower premature drug leakage than drug-loaded DDSs generated via noncovalent bonds, electrostatic interactions, hydrogen bonds, hydrophobic interactions, etc (Ulbrich et al., 2016). Once the polymer prodrugs are internalized into the tumor cells, these dynamic covalent conjugations can be cleaved to release the chemotherapeutic molecules, triggered by the acidic medium (endosomes and lysosomes: pH 5.0–6.2) and high reductant level (GSH at mM concentrations) in the tumor intracellular microenvironment. Thus, toxic side effects on normal cells and tissues are efficiently minimized, and enhanced antitumor efficacy is anticipated.

Recently, fluorescent materials, such as organic molecules and quantum dots, have been introduced into DDSs as tumor theranostic agents (Etrych et al., 2019), thus rendering the biodistribution of DDSs visible by fluorescence imaging. However, most of the reported tumor theranostic agents show the same fluorescence color in both normal physiological medium and the tumor intracellular microenvironment (Feng et al., 2016a; Gong et al., 2016; Hua et al., 2017, 2018; Jeong et al., 2020; Jia et al., 2016a, 2016b; Jung et al., 2015; Li et al., 2016a, 2016b, 2019; Lv et al., 2016; Santiago et al., 2014; Singh et al., 2017; Wang, et al., 2013; Yao et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2017; Zhou et al., 2013). Among these theranostic agents, some can produce the same fluorescence color at different fluorescence intensities in different media owing to the tumor specific pH- (Chen et al., 2014; Feng et al., 2016b; Gao et al., 2017; Jia et al., 2015, 2016c, 2016d; Pei et al., 2018a, 2018b, 2019a, 2019b; Zhang et al., 2016, 2018; Zhao et al., 2016; Zheng et al., 2015), redox- (Jiao et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2014) or enzyme- (Huang et al., 2015; Yildiz et al., 2018) responsive nature of the fluorescence. Differences in fluorescence intensity with the same fluorescence color could also be caused by the concentration of the fluorescent material. Thus, variations in fluorescence intensity are rarely used for real-time drug release monitoring and imaging.

Most recently, the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process, in which energy is transferred from a fluorophore in an electronic-excited state serving as a donor, to another chromophore or acceptor by long-range dipole–dipole coupling, has been used as a multicolor imaging technique for the real-time monitoring of drug release, by incorporation of a pair of donor/acceptor fluorophores with latent fluorogenic linkers (Cao et al., 2016; Hu and Zeng, 2017; Redy-Keisar et al., Yang, et al., 2015). Owing to the nature of macromolecular prodrugs, such theranostic prodrugs exhibit much less premature drug leakage than the small molecule nanodrug (Wang et al., 2016) or those generated via noncovalent drug-loading (Dong et al., 2019; Fu et al., 2016; Geng et al., 2018; Han et al., 2015; Qiu et al., 2014; Xing et al., 2014) (>30%). In fact, FRET is very sensitive in monitoring changes in the donor-to-acceptor separation distance at the nanoscale (generally less than 10 nm). Therefore, the design of FRET-based macromolecular theranostic prodrugs faces the following bottlenecks: control of the donor/acceptor proportion and selection of the proper fluorogenic linker. Furthermore, the use of dense fluorophores leads to fluorescence self-quenching.

In the present work, for the first time, a FRET-based macromolecular theranostic prodrug was designed by directly conjugating DOX as the FRET acceptor onto the naphthalimide (NAP) side groups in the fluorescent copolymer PPEGMA20-PNAP8 as the FRET energy donor, via an acid-labile imine bond (Scheme 1 ). The obtained polymer prodrug, PPEGMA20-PNAP8-DOX, showed a DOX content of 24.3% and could self-assemble into unique near-spherical nanoparticles approximately 71 nm. As a tumor theranostic agent, the proposed PPEGMA20-PNAP8-DOX nanoparticles showed excellent pH-triggered drug release and colorful fluorescence variations during drug delivery and release into tumor cells.

4-Bromo-1,8-naphthalic anhydride (99%) and p-hydroxybenzaldehyde (99%) were purchased from Tianjin Heowns Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Methacryloyl chloride (95%) and polyethylene glycol monomethyl ether methacrylate (PEGMA, MW ~ 475) were obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Ethanolamine (99%) was received from the Tianjin Guangfu Chemical Reagent Research Institute. Anhydrous potassium carbonate (99%) and 2,2′-azobis (2-methylpropionitrile) (AIBN, 98%) were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX·HCl, 99.4%) was purchased from Beijing Huafeng United Technology Co., Ltd. All other reagents were of an analytical grade and used directly. Double distilled water was used throughout the experiments.

Compound 1 was synthesized by refluxing 4-bromo-1, 8-naphthalic anhydride (5.52 g, 20 mmol) and ethanolamine (2.60 g, 43 mmol) in 100 mL of ethanol for 4 h (Zhou et al., 2016). After cooling to room temperature, the precipitate was collected by filtration and then washed with ethanol three times, and compound 1 was obtained after drying with a yield of 87%. 1H NMR (400 MHz, DMSO‑d 6, Fig. S1) δ 8.49 (dd, J = 15.0, 7.8 Hz, 2H), 8.32 – 8.22 (m, 1H), 8.16 (t, J = 5.8 Hz, 1H), 8.01 – 7.86 (m, 1H), 4.83 (s, 1H), 4.13 (t, J = 6.4 Hz, 2H), 3.74 – 3.52 (m, 2H).

Compound 2 was synthesized by heating compound 1 (2.32 g, 7.2 mmol), p-hydroxybenzaldehyde (1.06 g, 8.7 mmol) and anhydrous potassium carbonate (2.0 g, 14.4 mmol) in 50 mL of anhydrous DMF at 100 °C for 10 h (Kim et al., 2015). Then the mixture was poured into a mixture of ethyl acetate (50 mL) and H2O (50 mL). The aqueous phase was washed three times with ethyl acetate (50 mL × 3) and the organic phases were combined. Then, the organic layer was washed with saturated brine and water three times each and dried with anhydrous MgSO4. The crude product of compound 2 was obtained after the solvent was removed under reduced pressure and used directly for the next step without further purification.

Compound 2 (1.00 g, 1.7 mmol) and triethylamine (0.91 g, 9 mmol) were dissolved in CH2Cl2 (20 mL) and the solution was cooled to 0 °C. Then, 5.0 mL of a methylacryloyl chloride (0.94 g, 9 mmol) solution in dichloromethane was added dropwise over 1 h, and the reaction was conducted with stirring for 6 h at room temperature (Mao et al., 2016). Afterwards, the resultant solution was poured into water and the organic layer was separated, washed with saturated aqueous Na2CO3 and water three times each and dried with anhydrous MgSO4 overnight. The organic layer was concentrated to obtain the crude product as a yellow oil. Compound 3 was purified by column chromatography using silica gel as the stationary phase and a mixture of ethyl acetate/hexane (1:4) as the eluent. The pure product was obtained as a plain yellow powder with a yield of 74.6%. 1H NMR (400 MHz, CDCl3, Fig. S2) δ 10.02 (s, 1H), 8.68 (dd, J = 7.3, 1.1 Hz, 1H), 8.62–8.44 (m, 2H), 8.03–7.92 (m, 2H), 7.80 (dd, J = 8.4, 7.4 Hz, 1H), 7.31–7.26 (m, 2H), 7.13 (d, J = 8.2 Hz, 1H), 6.06 (s, 1H), 5.54–5.48 (m, 1H), 4.57 (t, J = 5.0 Hz, 2H), 4.50 (t, J = 5.0 Hz, 2H), 1.88 (s, 3H).

S-1-Dodecyl-S′-(α,α′-dimethyl-α″-acetic acid) trithiocarbonate (DDMAT) was synthesized as a macro-chain transfer agent for reversible addition–fragmentation chain transfer (RAFT) polymerization performed according to a previously reported method (Jia et al., 2015).

Then, the PPEGMA20-PNAP8 copolymer was synthesized via RAFT polymerization, as follows: PEGMA (380 mg, 0.8 mmol), DDMAT (44 mg, 0.12 mmol), AIBN (3.28 mg, 0.02 mmol), and compound 3 (172 mg, 0.4 mmol) were dissolved into 5.0 mL of DMF in a glass tube. After three freeze–pumpthaw cycles, the tube was sealed, and the polymerization was performed in an oil bath at 70 °C for 24 h. The product was collected as a yellow sticky oil, by precipitation in cold ether three times and dried under vacuum overnight.

DOX·HCl (50 mg) and triethylamine (100 μL) were added to the PPEGMA20-PNAP8 (50 mg) solution in 10 mL of DMSO in the dark. After the mixture had been stirred for 24 h at room temperature, it was dialyzed against DMSO to remove free DOX and then PBS (pH 7.4) for 24 h (MWCO = 1000 Da). The prodrug nanoparticles were obtained as a dark red powder after freeze-drying.
1H NMR spectra (proton nuclear magnetic resonance) were obtained on a JEOL ECS 400 MHz NMR spectrometer.
The morphology of the prodrug nanoparticles was characterized on a JEM1200 EX/S transmission electron microscope (TEM).
The hydrodynamic diameter and distribution of the prodrug nanoparticles were analyzed on a BI-200SM device (Brook Haven Instrument Company, USA) using dynamic light scattering (DLS) technology.
The fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer.

pH-triggered DOX release was performed at 37 °C as follows: 10.0 mL of a dispersion of PPEGMA20-PNAP8-DOX prodrug nanoparticles (5.0 mg) in a buffered solution (PBS (pH 7.4) or ABS (pH 5.0)) was transferred into a dialysis bag (MWCO: 1000 Da). Then, the dispersion was dialyzed against the corresponding buffered solution (100.0 mL). Five millilitres of each dialysate was removed to measure the DOX concentration with a TU-1901 UV–vis spectrophotometer at 480 nm, and 5.0 mL of the fresh buffered solution was added to keep the dialysate volume constant.

Cytotoxicity was evaluated in vitro via MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide) assay in the HepG2 cell line. After the cells had been cultivated in 96-well plates (1.0 × 105 cells per well) and incubated for 24 h, they were coincubated with the PPEGMA20-PNAP8-DOX prodrug nanoparticles, free DOX or blank PPEGMA20-PNAP8 nanoparticles at various concentrations for different times. Then, 20.0 μL of an MTT solution (5.0 mg/mL) was added to each well, followed by incubation for additional 4 h. Finally, the MTT solution was removed, and 150 μL of DMSO was added to dissolve the insoluble formazan crystals. Cell viability was detected by measuring absorbance at 490 nm on a microplate absorbance reader (Bio-Rad Laboratories, iMark).

Cellular uptake of the PPEGMA20-PNAP8-DOX prodrug nanoparticles into HepG2 cells was assessed via CLSM. After coincubation of HepG2 cells with 15 μg/mL PPEGMA20-PNAP8-DOX nanoparticles for 24 h, the culture medium was discarded, and the cells were washed with culture solution three times to remove the prodrug residue. Finally, the cells were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde, and the nuclei were stained with DAPI. Fluorescence images were obtained using an inverted fluorescence microscope (IX-71, Olympus).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the polymer prodrug PPEGMA20-PNAP8-DOX
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Scheme 1, the fluorescent copolymer PPEGMA20-PNAP8 containing naphthalimide side groups as the FRET energy donor was synthesized via the RAFT polymerization of PEGMA and compound 3 as comonomers, with AIBN and DDMAT as the initiator and chain transfer agent, respectively. In the 1H NMR spectrum of the PPEGMA20-PNAP8 copolymer (Fig. 1 a), the polymerization degrees of PEGMA and compound 3 were 20 and 8 respectively, as determined by calculating the ratio of the integral area of the characteristic methyl proton signals of PEGMA (δ = 3.34 ppm) and the benzaldehyde proton signal of compound 3 (δ = 9.96 ppm) to the proton of -C12H25 (δ = 1.15–1.28 ppm). As a result, the molecular weight of the PPEGMA20-PNAP8 copolymer was calculated to be 1.24 × 104. This agreed well with the GPC result of 1.27 × 104 with a unimodal and symmetric elution peak (Fig. S3).

Then the FRET-based macromolecular theranostic prodrug PPEGMA20-PNAP8-DOX was synthesized by directly conjugating DOX as the FRET acceptor onto the naphthalimide side groups in the fluorescent copolymer PPEGMA20-PNAP8 as the FRET energy donor, via an acid-labile imine bond (Scheme 1). In the 1H NMR spectrum of the product, the proton signals at δ = 5.35, 4.6 and 1.25 ppm corresponding to the t, k and s protons in DOX demonstrated the successful conjugation of DOX via an imine bond (Fuller et al., 2019), although the aldehyde groups in the fluorescent copolymer had not been completely conjugated because the proton signal of the aldehyde group could still be seen at δ = 10.0 ppm (Fig. 1b), perhaps due to spatial hindrance and/or reaction equilibrium in the formation of the dynamic covalent bond.

After thorough dialysis against DMSO to remove the free DOX, the DOX content in the resultant FRET-based macromolecular theranostic prodrug was determined to be approximately 24.3%, meaning that 7.63 naphthalimide side groups per fluorescent copolymer had been conjugated with DOX, by measuring the molar concentration of DOX on a UV–vis spectrometer at 480 nm compared with a standard curve of DOX in DMSO (Li and Liu, 2018). The result was near the theoretical drug content of 25.93%, which assumed that all the naphthalimide side groups in the fluorescent copolymer had been conjugated with DOX. These results demonstrated the high conjugation efficiency of > 95% in the proposed method.

Owing to the amphiphilicity of the polymer prodrug, due to its hydrophilic PEG blocks and the hydrophobic fluorescent prodrug groups, PPEGMA20-PNAP8-DOX could self-assemble into nanoparticles by dialysis of its DMSO solution against PBS (pH 7.4). In the TEM analysis (Fig. 2 a), nearly spherical nanoparticles with an average particle size of approximately 20 nm were seen. By DLS analysis, the mean hydrodynamic diameter (Dh) was determined to be approximately 71 nm with a very narrow distribution (PDI: 0.103) (Fig. 2b). The hydrodynamic diameter was much larger than the particle size determined by TEM analysis, due to swelling of the PEG brushes in the polymer prodrug. The results also revealed the successful self-assembly of the macromolecular theranostic prodrug PPEGMA20-PNAP8-DOX with hydrophobic fluorescent prodrug units as the core and hydrophilic PEG blocks as the shell. Such a desirable hydrodynamic diameter is expected to prevent fast systemic/renal clearance and therefore ensure passive tumor targeting through an enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro pH-triggered DOX release from PPEGMA20-PNAP8-DOX theranostic nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The in vitro acid-triggered release of DOX from the PPEGMA20-PNAP8-DOX theranostic nanoparticles was investigated in PBS (pH 7.4) and ABS (pH 5.0) buffer solutions at 37 °C, mimicking the normal physiological medium and tumor intracellular microenvironment, respectively. The PPEGMA20-PNAP8-DOX theranostic nanoparticles obviously possessed excellent pH-triggered drug release performance (Fig. 3 a). Owing to the stability of the acid-labile imine bond in the weak base medium (pH 7.4), premature drug leakage was evidently suppressed, with a low cumulative release rate of 6.3% over 129 h. As in the acidic medium (pH 5.0), drug release was obviously accelerated without burst release, owing to acid-triggered cleavage of the imine bond (Fuller et al., 2019). The cumulative release rate reached 59.7% over 24 h and 91.3% over 129 h. The in vitro release results indicated the potential application of the proposed theranostic nanoparticles in tumor chemotherapy.

To further explore the drug release mechanism, the hydrodynamic diameter of the theranostic nanoparticles was tracked with DLS during drug release in ABS (pH 5.0). As shown in Fig. 3b, the hydrodynamic diameter went from unimodal to bimodal mode within 2 h: one in the range of 85–250 nm and the other in the range of 43–80 nm. After 5 h, the peak corresponding to nanoparticles with a large diameter retained, while the peak corresponding to a small diameter shifted to a smaller region at approximately 50 nm. Both peaks shifted to the smaller region after 10 h. With an increased observation time of 120 h, only a very narrow peak at 20–25 nm remained. This phenomenon indicated that the theranostic nanoparticles had disintegrated and re-self-assembled (Zhang and Liu, 2020), due to variations in hydrophilic/hydrophobic balance during drug release. Additionally, owing to disintegration of the PPEGMA20-PNAP8-DOX nanoparticles and re-self-assembly of the resultant amphiphilic PEGMA20-PNAP8 molecules during drug release, a high cumulative release rate was achieved with the amphiphilic fluorescent copolymer as the backbone of the proposed macromolecular theranostic prodrug.

The fluorescent property of the backbone copolymer PPEGMA20-PNAP8 was first evalutaed. It emitted blue fluorescence at 446 nm with excitation at 380 nm (Fig. 4 a). In addition, the fluorescence intensity showed no distinct change at different pH values from 4 to 9 (Fig. 4c). Intramolecular FRET occurred after DOX was conjugated by the addition of a 5.0 mg/mL DOX solution in DMSO to 3.0 mL of a 0.70 mg/mL PPEGMA20-PNAP8 solution in DMSO. Fluorescence spectra were recorded after addition of the DOX solution and stirring for 30 min. When the DOX content increased, the blue fluorescence intensity (I446 nm) gradually decreased and the orange fluorescence intensity (I596nm) gradually increased (Fig. 4a). The relative fluorescence intensity ratio (FRET signal R, where R = I596nm / I446nm) reached a maximum of 16.18 with a DOX content of 0.75 mg (1.38 × 10-3 mmol). The mass of the fluorescent copolymer was 2.10 mg in solution, meaning that the number of the naphthalimide side groups in the solution was 1.35 × 10-3 mmol. These results indicated that FRET process was achieved with a molar donor/acceptor ratio of 1:1 via both covalent conjugation and noncovalent interaction.

In acid-triggered DOX release, FRET was investigated by dialyzing a 10 mL dispersion of PEGMA20-PNAP8-DOX theranostic nanoparticles (5.0 mg) in ABS (pH 5.0) against ABS (pH 5.0) with a dialysis bag (MWCO: 1000 Da) at 37 °C. After a certain duration, 3.0 mL of the dispersion was removed for fluorescence measurement and then recycled. The fluorescence spectra are presented in Fig. 4b. With prolonged dialysis time, the orange fluorescence intensity (I596nm) decreased gradually, while the blue fluorescence intensity increased, because the conjugated DOX had been cleaved off and moved out of the dialysis bag. After 120 h, at which time the cumulative DOX release rate was approximately 90%, the blue fluorescence of the naphthalimide side groups had recovered.

The above results demonstrated that the blue fluorescence of the backbone copolymer PPEGMA20-PNAP8 changed to the orange fluorescence of the macromolecular theranostic prodrug PPEGMA20-PNAP8-DOX, after the conjugation of DOX via an imine bond. The PPEGMA20-PNAP8-DOX theranostic nanoparticles were stable in normal physiological medium (like circulating blood) with orange fluorescence. Once the theranostic nanoparticles had been internalized into the tumor cells, the conjugated DOX was cleaved off in the acidic tumor intracellular microenvironment, and the blue fluorescence of the backbone copolymer in the nanoparticles was restored. That is, the proposed theranostic nanoparticles could emit fluorescence with different colors in normal tissue and tumor tissue. Such variations in fluorescence color during intracellular drug delivery and release are expected for real-time tumor intracellular drug release monitoring and imaging diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cellular toxicity and uptake
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of the proposed PPEGMA20-PNAP8-DOX theranostic nanoparticles was assessed by the MTT assay after HepG2 cells were incubated with the nanoparticles at different concentrations for 24 h, and these results were compared with those collected with the backbone copolymer PPEGMA20-PNAP8 and free DOX. As shown in Fig. 5 , the backbone copolymer PPEGMA20-PNAP8 did not show a distinct cytotoxic effect on the tumor cells even at a high concentration of 20 µg/mL, and a high cell viability of over 94% was also observed.

Like free DOX, the PPEGMA20-PNAP8-DOX theranostic nanoparticles exhibited dose-dependent toxicity. Cell viability after incubation with the theranostic nanoparticles was clearly lower than that with free DOX at the equivalent dose, especially at lower doses (Fig. 5a). These results indicated the enhanced antitumor efficacy of the proposed PPEGMA20-PNAP8-DOX theranostic nanoparticles compared to free DOX, owing to cellular endocytosis of the theranostic nanoparticles and intracellular pH-triggered drug release from the theranostic nanoparticles, and because the drug avoided the P-glycoprotein efflux pump (Pei et al., 2019a). At a high dose of 20 µg/mL, a cell viability of 29.7% was achieved with the theranostic nanoparticles, similar to that achieved with free DOX (29.5%). This is expected to be due to sustained DOX release from the theranostic nanoparticles, which showed a cumulative release rate of 59.7% over 24 h, while free DOX was released completely over a few hours (Zhang and Liu, 2019).

Because DOX does normally not have a great effect in vitro when incubation times are below 48 h, longer incubation times were also used, and the results were compared. After 48 h of incubation, a higher degree of tumor growth inhibition was obtained with free DOX (Fig. 5b). This phenomenon is in accordance with the reported work (Li et al., 2020). However, higher antitumor efficacy after 72 h of incubation was achieved with the proposed PPEGMA20-PNAP8-DOX theranostic nanoparticles than with free DOX (Fig. 5c).

The cellular internalization and intracellular biodistribution of the proposed PPEGMA20-PNAP8-DOX theranostic nanoparticles were also investigated with CLSM, after HepG2 cells were incubated with the theranostic nanoparticles (15 µg/mL) for 24 h. When the cellular nuclei were stained with DAPI, the red fluorescence of DOX, the blue fluorescence of DAPI (nuclei) and the yellow fluorescence of the naphthalimide groups were observed (Fig. 6 a-c). In the merged images (a + b and a + c), the blue fluorescence of DAPI (nuclei) overlapped with the red fluorescence of DOX and yellow fluorescence of the naphthalimide groups, indicating that the PPEGMA20-PNAP8-DOX theranostic nanoparticles had been successfully internalized into the HepG2 cells. Furthermore, DOX was released because of acid-triggered cleavage of the imine bond in the acidic intracellular medium. The released DOX and the fluorescent backbone copolymer PPEGMA20-PNAP8 mainly accumulated in the nuclei (Pei et al., 2019a).

To avoid possible interference from DAPI, CLSM images were also taken without DAPI staining. The HepG2 cells exhibited the red fluorescence of DOX and yellow-green fluorescence of the naphthalimide groups (Fig. 6d and e). The nuclei clearly showed much stronger fluorescence than the cell cytoplasm, also revealing that the two components had mainly accumulated in the nuclei. In addition, the two fluorescence regions were completely overlapped in the merged image (d + e), demonstrating the same intracellular biodistribution shown by the released DOX and fluorescent backbone copolymer PPEGMA20-PNAP8. Therefore, due to the variations in fluorescence color during drug delivery and release in the tumor cells, the proposed PPEGMA20-PNAP8-DOX theranostic nanoparticles show promising potential in real-time tumor intracellular drug release monitoring and imaging diagnosis.

In summary, a facile strategy to fabricate intramolecular FRET-based macromolecular theranostic prodrug nanoparticles for real-time tumor intracellular drug release monitoring and imaging diagnosis was developed, by directly conjugating DOX onto the NAP side groups in the backbone copolymer PPEGMA20-PNAP8 via an acid-labile imine bond without a fluorogenic linker. Owing to acid-labile imine conjugation between the drug and backbone copolymer, which act as the acceptor and donor in FRET respectively, the self-assembled PPEGMA20-PNAP8-DOX theranostic nanoparticles showed orange fluorescence and very low premature drug leakage in normal physiological medium, while the fluorescence was restored to the blue fluorescence of the backbone copolymer after pH-triggered drug release in the acidic tumor intracellular microenvironment. Such features, especially the excellent pH-triggered drug release and self-tracking variations in pH-responsive fluorescence color during intracellular drug delivery and release, indicate that the proposed PPEGMA20-PNAP8-DOX theranostic nanoparticles show promising potential for cancer therapy and diagnosis in the future.
Yuman Dong: Investigation, Data curation. Pengcheng Du: Investigation. Peng Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Visualization, Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijpharm.2020.119723.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanomedicine: An effective tool in cancer therapy

Biomacromolecules 17, 1496−1505.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Aptamer/graphene quantum dots nanocomposite capped fluorescent mesoporous silica nanoparticles for intracellular drug delivery and real-time monitoring of drug release

Aptamer/graphene quantum dots nanocomposite capped fluorescent mesoporous silica nanoparticles for intracellular drug delivery and real-time monitoring of drug release. Anal. Chem. 87, 11739−11745.
